title: Search Insert Position
code: public static int SearchInsert(int[] nums, int target)

frames.caption: Initialize the half-open window [lo, hi) = [0, 4). hi is exclusive — index 4 means "insert at the end."
frames.note: Search Insert Position is *exactly* lower bound: the first index i with nums[i] >= target. That index is where the value already sits if present, and where it would be inserted to keep order if absent — so no special-casing is ever needed. We use the half-open interval [lo, hi): lo is the leftmost candidate, hi is exclusive and starts at nums.Length (a valid "insert at the end" answer). The invariant is that the boundary always lives in [lo, hi].

frames.caption: mid = 0 + (4 - 0)/2 = 2. Probe nums[2] = 5 against target 5.
frames.note: Compute mid as lo + (hi - lo) / 2, never (lo + hi) / 2 — the naive sum can overflow a 32-bit int. Integer division floors toward lo, which matters for the half-open template: with the converging branches lo = mid + 1 and hi = mid, flooring toward lo is exactly what guarantees the interval keeps shrinking and the loop cannot spin forever.

frames.caption: nums[2] = 5 >= 5: mid is a candidate. Keep it, shrink from the right: hi = mid = 2.
frames.note: Here nums[mid] >= target, so mid itself might be the boundary — the very first index >= target. The half-open template treats hi as exclusive, so hi = mid keeps mid as a live candidate while shrinking the window. This is the crux: writing hi = mid - 1 instead would discard the answer whenever mid is the boundary.

frames.caption: mid = 0 + (2 - 0)/2 = 1. Probe nums[1] = 3 against target 5.
frames.note: Compute mid as lo + (hi - lo) / 2, never (lo + hi) / 2 — the naive sum can overflow a 32-bit int. Integer division floors toward lo, which matters for the half-open template: with the converging branches lo = mid + 1 and hi = mid, flooring toward lo is exactly what guarantees the interval keeps shrinking and the loop cannot spin forever.

frames.caption: nums[1] = 3 < 5: too small. Discard the left half incl. mid: lo = mid + 1 = 2.
frames.note: The predicate is nums[mid] < target — "still too small, go right." Because the array is sorted, every index at or left of mid is also < target, so none of them can be the first index >= target. Discarding that whole half with lo = mid + 1 is safe, and stepping past mid (not back to it) is what makes the interval strictly shrink.

frames.caption: lo == hi == 2: the window collapsed onto the boundary. Return 2 — the insert position.
frames.note: The loop ends when lo == hi: the window has collapsed onto a single index, and by the invariant that index is the boundary — the first position >= target. For nums = [1,3,5,6], target = 5, the answer is 2 (target is present at index 2). The same code returns 1 for target 2 and 4 for target 7 (insert at the end) — all just lower-bound results, no extra cases.
